category_summary:
The AI community buzzed around a massive viral thread from **Anthropic** engineer **Boris Cherny** [revealing 15 hidden **Claude Code** features](/?date=2026-03-31&category=social#item-9b7b93c7060a)—including mobile coding, cross-device teleportation, autonomous scheduling loops, and parallel batch agents—amassing 21K likes and 3.2M views.

- **Tri Dao** (FlashAttention creator) [shared deep linear algebra insights](/?date=2026-03-31&category=social#item-a3ef9461f32b) on speeding up the **Muon optimizer**, showcasing frontier ML optimization work
- **Clément Delangue** [announced **GGML** officially joining](/?date=2026-03-31&category=social#item-0cca4651f99a) **Hugging Face**, a landmark moment for the open-source AI ecosystem
- **Ethan Mollick** dominated discourse with multiple high-signal posts: an original analysis [arguing **Jevon's Paradox** oversimplifies](/?date=2026-03-31&category=social#item-16fefdd8e374) AI economics since agent-driven token demand outpaces efficiency gains; commentary on [**ARC-AGI-3** benchmark progression](/?date=2026-03-31&category=social#item-db1a9343c48b); concrete **NBER** [productivity data showing 6% time savings](/?date=2026-03-31&category=social#item-dec9bd4b2364) for AI-using workers; and a viral thought experiment on why the first lab to build ASI [would keep it secret](/?date=2026-03-31&category=social#item-27491f86147d) to exploit financial markets

**François Chollet** [endorsed **PokeeClaw**](/?date=2026-03-31&category=social#item-05b7c86bcb0f) as a secure alternative to **OpenClaw**, addressing critical agent security concerns. **Allie K Miller** [reacted to a buried **Anthropic** tweet](/?date=2026-03-31&category=social#item-bc1f4b59ec44) showcasing Claude's coding capabilities, noting Fortune 500 executives are already rethinking entire engineering teams. **Hugging Face** CEO [called for open-source agent tools](/?date=2026-03-31&category=social#item-a724334d1ef9) to stop depending on closed-source APIs.
category_summary_html:
The AI community buzzed around a massive viral thread from Anthropic engineer Boris Cherny revealing 15 hidden Claude Code features—including mobile coding, cross-device teleportation, autonomous scheduling loops, and parallel batch agents—amassing 21K likes and 3.2M views.

  • Tri Dao (FlashAttention creator) shared deep linear algebra insights on speeding up the Muon optimizer, showcasing frontier ML optimization work
  • Clément Delangue announced GGML officially joining Hugging Face, a landmark moment for the open-source AI ecosystem
  • Ethan Mollick dominated discourse with multiple high-signal posts: an original analysis arguing Jevon's Paradox oversimplifies AI economics since agent-driven token demand outpaces efficiency gains; commentary on ARC-AGI-3 benchmark progression; concrete NBER productivity data showing 6% time savings for AI-using workers; and a viral thought experiment on why the first lab to build ASI would keep it secret to exploit financial markets

François Chollet endorsed PokeeClaw as a secure alternative to OpenClaw, addressing critical agent security concerns. Allie K Miller reacted to a buried Anthropic tweet showcasing Claude's coding capabilities, noting Fortune 500 executives are already rethinking entire engineering teams. Hugging Face CEO called for open-source agent tools to stop depending on closed-source APIs.

themes.name: Claude Code Power Features
themes.description: Massive viral thread from Anthropic's Boris Cherny revealing 15 hidden/under-utilized Claude Code features including mobile app, teleport, loops/scheduling, hooks, batch parallelization, voice coding, custom agents, and worktrees. Combined with Anthropic engineer trq212 investigating prompt cache bugs and usage issues.

themes.name: AI Economics & Pricing
themes.description: Discussion of AI compute constraints, token demand from agents, model pricing dynamics, and the economics of frontier vs. cheap models.

themes.name: Anthropic Claude Capabilities Breakthrough
themes.description: Multiple posts react to what appears to be a major Anthropic announcement about Claude's GUI-building and coding capabilities, with commentators calling it game-changing and noting scaling laws are holding.

themes.name: Recursive Self-Improvement & AI Training Automation
themes.description: Claims that AI labs like HF0 are letting AI manage its own training loops, with Scobleizer suggesting this represents the beginning of recursive self-improvement. Raises significant AI safety implications.

themes.name: Open-Source AI & Local Models
themes.description: Hugging Face strategy, GGML joining HF, open-source agent tools, scaffolding challenges for local models, and the push for AI builders over API users.

themes.name: Training Optimization & Technical Research
themes.description: Tri Dao's Muon optimizer speedup through linear algebra insights, mechanistic interpretability workshop.

themes.name: AI Productivity & Workforce Impact
themes.description: Data on AI time savings for workers, US productivity advantages, and how current measurements predate practical agents.

themes.name: Claude Code Scaling & Growing Pains
themes.description: Anthropic engineer Boris Cherny publicly acknowledges Claude Code's rapid growth is straining services, actively triaging user-reported bugs and performance issues. Multiple improvement releases landing.

themes.name: AI Agents & Security (OpenClaw/PokeeClaw)
themes.description: OpenClaw's product-market fit, security concerns, and PokeeClaw as a secure alternative with sandboxing and RBAC.

themes.name: AI Productivity and Economic Impact
themes.description: Ethan Mollick shares NBER research showing American AI users save ~6% of work time (2.5 hrs/week), with early signs of productivity growth. Notes these measurements predate practical AI agents like Claude Code, suggesting impacts may grow.

items.title:
It's my favorite kind of work: linear algebra insight + fast kernels.
When playing w Muon a while ag...
items.content:
It's my favorite kind of work: linear algebra insight + fast kernels.
When playing w Muon a while ago, we were thinking why not speed it up by operating on the small square matrix X X^T instead of the large rectangular matrix X. Jack, Noah, and Berlin spent many months understanding eigenvalues/vectors of the intermediate matrices in Muon, and finally came up with a simple and elegant algo to make this work.
items.author: @tri_dao
items.summary: Tri Dao describes work on speeding up Muon optimizer by operating on the small square matrix X X^T instead of the large rectangular X, requiring months of understanding eigenvalues/eigenvectors to develop a simple, elegant algorithm.
items.reasoning: High-quality technical content from Tri Dao (FlashAttention creator). Very high engagement (921 likes, 63K views). Describes novel optimization work combining linear algebra insights with fast kernels. This is significant for training efficiency.
items.summary_html: Tri Dao describes work on speeding up Muon optimizer by operating on the small square matrix X X^T instead of the large rectangular X, requiring months of understanding eigenvalues/eigenvectors to develop a simple, elegant algorithm.

items.title: It is trendy to discuss Jevon's Paradox in AI (as AI gets more efficient, overall use increases) but...
items.content: It is trendy to discuss Jevon's Paradox in AI (as AI gets more efficient, overall use increases) but the current situation is much simpler: thanks to agents, token demand is surging and compute is supply constrained, at least for powerful models. That will be reflected in pricing
items.author: @emollick
items.summary: Emollick argues that discussion of Jevon's Paradox in AI is oversimplified - the real issue is that agent-driven token demand is surging while compute is supply-constrained for powerful models, which will be reflected in pricing.
items.reasoning: Excellent original analysis of AI economics. High engagement (326 likes, 21K views). The insight that agents are driving token demand surge and creating supply constraints for powerful models is highly relevant to the industry.
items.summary_html: Emollick argues that discussion of Jevon's Paradox in AI is oversimplified - the real issue is that agent-driven token demand is surging while compute is supply-constrained for powerful models, which will be reflected in pricing.

That will be reflected in pricing

items.title: This is true, but ARC-AGI-3 is also a test designed so that AI gets zero today, just as the earlier ...
items.content:
This is true, but ARC-AGI-3 is also a test designed so that AI gets zero today, just as the earlier ARC-AGI tests were designed . Those tests were then mostly saturated with a year or two.

The thing to watch with ARC-AGI-3 is whether we see the same progress.
items.author: @emollick
items.summary: Emollick comments on ARC-AGI-3, noting it's designed so AI scores zero today (just like earlier versions), and those earlier tests were mostly saturated within a year or two. The key question is whether we see similar progress on ARC-AGI-3.
items.reasoning: Important commentary on AI benchmark progression from a top voice. High engagement (292 likes, 33K views). Contextualizes the significance of ARC-AGI-3 and the pattern of rapid AI improvement on supposedly-hard benchmarks.
items.summary_html: Emollick comments on ARC-AGI-3, noting it's designed so AI scores zero today (just like earlier versions), and those earlier tests were mostly saturated within a year or two. The key question is whether we see similar progress on ARC-AGI-3.

This is true, but ARC-AGI-3 is also a test designed so that AI gets zero today, just as the earlier ARC-AGI tests were designed . Those tests were then mostly saturated with a year or two.

The thing to watch with ARC-AGI-3 is whether we see the same progress.

items.title: I was told by one researcher at @HF0 in San Francisco that they took "hands off" of their AI trainin...
items.content:
I was told by one researcher at @HF0 in San Francisco that they took "hands off" of their AI training. Now AI does it all. Has already increased in speed many times, he told me, since doing that. 

Think about an OpenClaw improving itself all night long.

Except on a much bigger scale.

Things are about to get weird in AI.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer claims a researcher at HF0 told him they took 'hands off' of AI training, letting AI do it all, with training speed increasing many times since. He extrapolates this to OpenClaw improving itself and predicts 'things are about to get weird in AI.'
items.reasoning: Highest engagement in this batch (276 likes, 25.6K views). Claims about AI-driven AI training (self-improvement loops) at HF0 are significant if true. This touches on recursive self-improvement — a key concern in AI safety. However, the claim is secondhand and from Scobleizer who tends toward hype. Still, the core signal about AI labs automating their own training processes is a major trend worth tracking.

items.title: OpenClaw has proven that local AI assistants have product-market fit. But the big issue with them ha...
items.content:
OpenClaw has proven that local AI assistants have product-market fit. But the big issue with them has been security.

The team at @Pokee_AI is fixing it with PokeeClaw: works like OpenClaw, but with in a secure sandbox architecture with isolated environments, approval workflows, role-based access control, and audit trails built in. Also lower token usage, which is always nice.
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: François Chollet announces PokeeClaw by Pokee_AI: a secure alternative to OpenClaw with sandbox architecture, isolated environments, approval workflows, RBAC, and audit trails. Also lower token usage.
items.reasoning: Major product endorsement from Chollet (ARC-AGI creator). OpenClaw security has been a known concern, and this addresses it directly. Very high engagement (361 likes, 65K views). Signals maturation of local AI assistant ecosystem.

items.title:
3/ Two of the most powerful features in Claude Code: /loop and /schedule

Use these to schedule Clau...
items.content:
3/ Two of the most powerful features in Claude Code: /loop and /schedule

Use these to schedule Claude to run automatically at a set interval, for up to a week at a time.

I have a bunch of loops running locally:

- /loop 5m /babysit, to auto-address code review, auto-rebase, and shepherd my PRs to production
- /loop 30m /slack-feedback, to automatically put up PRs for Slack feedback every 30 mins
- /loop /post-merge-sweeper to put up PRs to address code review comments I missed
- /loop 1h /pr-pruner to close out stale and no longer necessary PRs
- lots more!..

Experiment with turning workflows into skills + loops. It's powerful.

https://t.co/cx6YMibGN3
items.author: @bcherny
items.summary: bcherny details /loop and /schedule for automated Claude Code workflows: auto-babysitting PRs, Slack feedback loops, PR pruning, post-merge sweeping - all running continuously.
items.reasoning: Extremely high engagement (1455 likes, 266K views). Reveals a paradigm of continuous autonomous agent workflows managing the full PR lifecycle. This represents a significant shift in software development patterns.
items.summary_html: bcherny details /loop and /schedule for automated Claude Code workflows: auto-babysitting PRs, Slack feedback loops, PR pruning, post-merge sweeping - all running continuously.

I have a bunch of loops running locally:

  • /loop 30m /slack-feedback, to automatically put up PRs for Slack feedback every 30 mins

items.title:
This is an insane Anthropic tweet.

And it’s a *buried reply* to one of their other tweets. 

I am r...
items.content:
This is an insane Anthropic tweet.

And it’s a *buried reply* to one of their other tweets. 

I am reminded of a talk I gave ~2-3 months ago where a senior developer at a Fortune 500 company asked me “why would I use AI to code if I can just code myself.” 

I answered. 

He said, “But sometimes it messes up.”

I told him this was coming. Even if it’s not perfect today (it makes weird product features decisions sometimes, not gonna lie), the scaling laws seem to be holding up this year and the next iteration will be even more capable. 

I wish I could send him this tweet.

items.title: The easiest way to make money fast from a superhuman artificial intelligence would be in the financi...
items.content: The easiest way to make money fast from a superhuman artificial intelligence would be in the financial markets, almost by definition. So the first lab to develop one, if AGI is possible, would almost certainly keep it quiet for as long as they could. Beats charging for API access

items.title: In a world where everyone can build websites, apps and features easily (thank you Cursor, Lovable, C...
items.content:
In a world where everyone can build websites, apps and features easily (thank you Cursor, Lovable, Claude and the likes), it will take more for you and your company to differentiate themselves (which is in my opinion the basis for success). 

That's why we're seeing more and more people and companies starting to train, optimize and run their own models (rather than outsource this to third parties). 

This is the future we want to enable with Hugging Face: empower millions of people to build AI themselves, not just be API users. 

Cool new project in this vein from @mishig25: auto-research built on top of @huggingface so that your agents find and push their intermediary checkpoints, datasets, learn from papers and collaborate on the hub: https://t.co/YWCzp5ZIfC

Let's make all AI builders rather than AI users!
items.author: @ClementDelangue
items.summary: Clement argues that as everyone can build apps easily with AI tools, differentiation requires training/running your own models. Promotes HF's vision of enabling people to be AI builders, not just API users. Shares auto-research project.
items.reasoning: Strategic vision from HF CEO about the future of AI differentiation. High engagement (435 likes, 47K views). Articulates an important thesis about the commoditization of app-building and the value shifting to custom models.
items.summary_html: Clement argues that as everyone can build apps easily with AI tools, differentiation requires training/running your own models. Promotes HF's vision of enabling people to be AI builders, not just API users. Shares auto-research project.

In a world where everyone can build websites, apps and features easily (thank you Cursor, Lovable, Claude and the likes), it will take more for you and your company to differentiate themselves (which is in my opinion the basis for success).

That's why we're seeing more and more people and companies starting to train, optimize and run their own models (rather than outsource this to third parties).

This is the future we want to enable with Hugging Face: empower millions of people to build AI themselves, not just be API users.

Cool new project in this vein from @mishig25: auto-research built on top of @huggingface so that your agents find and push their intermediary checkpoints, datasets, learn from papers and collaborate on the hub: https://t.co/YWCzp5ZIfC

items.title:
Exclusive: MiniMax AI developer report.

TL;DR: It provides more performance for a much lower price....
items.content:
Exclusive: MiniMax AI developer report.

TL;DR: It provides more performance for a much lower price.

I now do custom reports for companies with the analysis engine that @blevlabs and I built over the past couple of months. You've seen my reports on OpenClaw and other technologies. Here I did one for MiniMax, comparing it to other models: https://t.co/WhnARBnU8c

This report was written by using the X API to grab tens of thousands of posts from the AI community (I have the most complete lists of such anywhere: https://t.co/9eRY65x3IQ and Levangie Labs' cognitive architecture does the best job of putting it all together in a report that I've found). 

Key things from the report:

++"MiniMax has built the most cost-effective frontier-tier coding and agentic model on the market."

++"Architecture: MoE (Mixture of Experts) — 230B total parameters, 10B active. This is the key insight: you get frontier-tier reasoning with the inference cost of a 10B model."

++"What the community said: "Basically Claude Opus performance but 95% cheaper." "80.2% SWE-Bench. 76.8% on agentic tool-calling. Genuinely underrated." One developer burned through 922 million tokens in 3 days on the coding plan across 50+ parallel sessions."

++"The consensus: MiniMax M2.7 matches or exceeds Opus on coding and agentic tasks at 1/50th the price."

++"Bottom Line for Developers: If you're building coding agents, agentic workflows, or any system that makes a lot of API calls, MiniMax is the most important model to know right now. The price-to-performance ratio is genuinely unprecedented at the frontier tier."

The complete report comparing it to the other models: https://t.co/WhnARBnU8c

Are you using MiniMax? Why not? The AI community here on X is.

MiniMax Agent → https://t.co/NWX9GThijF
API → https://t.co/lPc0F11xOU
Token Plan → https://t.co/EDr6dR38w1
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer publishes a detailed developer report on MiniMax M2.7, claiming it matches Claude Opus performance at 1/50th the price. Key specs: 230B total params, 10B active (MoE), 80.2% SWE-Bench, 76.8% agentic tool-calling. Report generated using X API data and Levangie Labs' analysis engine.

items.summary_html: Scobleizer publishes a detailed developer report on MiniMax M2.7, claiming it matches Claude Opus performance at 1/50th the price. Key specs: 230B total params, 10B active (MoE), 80.2% SWE-Bench, 76.8% agentic tool-calling. Report generated using X API data and Levangie Labs' analysis engine.
items.content_html:
Exclusive: MiniMax AI developer report.

TL;DR: It provides more performance for a much lower price.

I now do custom reports for companies with the analysis engine that @blevlabs and I built over the past couple of months. You've seen my reports on OpenClaw and other technologies. Here I did one for MiniMax, comparing it to other models: https://t.co/WhnARBnU8c

This report was written by using the X API to grab tens of thousands of posts from the AI community (I have the most complete lists of such anywhere: https://t.co/9eRY65x3IQ and Levangie Labs' cognitive architecture does the best job of putting it all together in a report that I've found).

Key things from the report:

++"MiniMax has built the most cost-effective frontier-tier coding and agentic model on the market."

++"Architecture: MoE (Mixture of Experts) — 230B total parameters, 10B active. This is the key insight: you get frontier-tier reasoning with the inference cost of a 10B model."

++"What the community said: "Basically Claude Opus performance but 95% cheaper." "80.2% SWE-Bench. 76.8% on agentic tool-calling. Genuinely underrated." One developer burned through 922 million tokens in 3 days on the coding plan across 50+ parallel sessions."

++"The consensus: MiniMax M2.7 matches or exceeds Opus on coding and agentic tasks at 1/50th the price."

++"Bottom Line for Developers: If you're building coding agents, agentic workflows, or any system that makes a lot of API calls, MiniMax is the most important model to know right now. The price-to-performance ratio is genuinely unprecedented at the frontier tier."

The complete report comparing it to the other models: https://t.co/WhnARBnU8c

Are you using MiniMax? Why not? The AI community here on X is.

MiniMax Agent → https://t.co/NWX9GThijF

API → https://t.co/lPc0F11xOU

Token Plan → https://t.co/EDr6dR38w1

4/ Use hooks to deterministically run logic as part of the agent lifecycle

items.content:
4/ Use hooks to deterministically run logic as part of the agent lifecycle

For example, use hooks to:
- Dynamically load in context each time you start Claude (SessionStart)
- Log every bash command the model runs (PreToolUse)
- Route permission prompts to WhatsApp for you to approve/deny (PermissionRequest)
- Poke Claude to keep going whenever it stops (Stop)

See https://t.co/GukTJ9iy0R

items.title: A lot of people mistake inadequate scaffolding for a lack of capability in smaller local models. You...
items.content:
A lot of people mistake inadequate scaffolding for a lack of capability in smaller local models. You can't expect a system that was built for large model APIs to work out of the box magically with smaller local models.

Similarly, many people fine-tune poorly and then conclude that fine-tuning does not work.

There are clearly still major skills gaps and time investment constraints with local model currently but I'm quite hopeful that a mix of open-source explosion, community work and agent support will make it drastically easier. 

And then we'll witness the open-source and local explosion that is so necessary!

items.summary: Clement argues people mistake poor scaffolding for lack of capability in smaller local models, and that poor fine-tuning leads to wrong conclusions. Expresses optimism about open-source explosion making local models easier.

There are clearly still major skills gaps and time investment constraints with local model currently but I'm quite hopeful that a mix of open-source explosion, community work and agent support will make it drastically easier.

items.content:
Whoever uses AI to write code should be ultimately responsible for that code and liable for any potential consequences of running that code.

I don't care what model you used, how you used it, or how much it helped you.

Humans bear full responsibility.

items.title:
6/ Use the Chrome extension for frontend work

The most important tip for using Claude Code is: give...
items.content:
6/ Use the Chrome extension for frontend work

The most important tip for using Claude Code is: give Claude a way to verify its output. Once you do that, Claude will iterate until the result is great.

Think of it like any other engineer: if you ask someone to build a website but they aren't allowed to use a browser, will the result look good? Probably not. But if you give them a browser, they will write code and iterate until it looks good.

Personally, I use the Chrome extension every time I work on web code. It tends to work more reliably than other similar MCPs.

Download the extension for Chrome/Edge here: https://t.co/m7wwQUmp1C

items.title:
Just saw a new agentic platform that goes way beyond the others I've seen. Coming in two weeks. 

I ...
items.content:
Just saw a new agentic platform that goes way beyond the others I've seen. Coming in two weeks. 

I have no idea how anyone keeps up without using AI now. 

Lesson I've taken away: everyone must be prepared to change as new things come.

Have your AI analyze new things, compare them to what you are using, and be able to help you change to new thing.

My developer changed from OpenClaw to Hermes in one night.

But he's 22. They are so damn fast at changing. 

Me? I'll be honest. I'm struggling. Where I'm struggling I'm building AI to take over. And everyone is struggling to deal with change and it'll get worse. Part of it is spiritual. I'm dopamine addicted thanks to social media that addicted me. Getting me off the need for constant dopamine hits is gonna be something, but am working on it. 

Those inside the bubble have both a huge advantage because of this, but a huge disadvantage. As they build AI systems to help them deal with change they are getting busier. This is hitting all execs and founders. As Jensen Huang, founder of NVIDIA said at GTC: he's constantly in the critical path. That's fun, but also going to cause a lot to burn out if they don't have agentic systems to help make decisions faster (and do things on their own).

I see a job boom coming for those who know how to setup agentic platforms fast and get to work on solving other people's struggles. 

I'm building something to help keep up with the AI world here on X, almost done. @blevlabs and I are meeting tonight to finish it off.

Cheap models are still getting cheaper, but the highest end models increasingly offer a combination of intelligence, judgement, planning and correctness that make using them, rather than cheap models, extremely valuable. 

And because they are valuable, companies can charge more.

items.content:
2/ Move sessions back and forth between mobile/web/desktop and terminal

Run "claude --teleport" or /teleport to continue a cloud session on your machine.

Or run /remote-control to control a locally running session from your phone/web. Personally, I have "Enable Remote Control for all sessions" set in my /config.

https://t.co/mJlFQdjm50

items.title: The problem with credit chasing around LLMs is that essentially all of the components existed: trans...
items.content: The problem with credit chasing around LLMs is that essentially all of the components existed: transformers, next-word prediction, transfer learning, scaling trends. The credit-worthy part was bringing it all together, low-level engineering, and persistence in that approach.
items.author: @andrewgwils
items.summary: Andrew Wilson argues the credit-worthy part of LLMs was bringing existing components together (transformers, next-word prediction, transfer learning, scaling) with low-level engineering and persistence, not any single invention.

items.title:
10/ Use git worktrees

Claude Code ships with deep support for git worktrees. Worktrees are essentia...
items.content:
10/ Use git worktrees

Claude Code ships with deep support for git worktrees. Worktrees are essential for doing lots of parallel work in the same repository. I have dozens of Claudes running at all times, and this is how I do it.

Use claude -w to start a new session in a worktree, or hit the "worktree" checkbox in the Claude Desktop app.

For non-git VCS users, use the WorktreeCreate hook to add your own logic for worktree creation.

Learn more: https://t.co/Yv3SlQr1Kr
items.author: @bcherny
items.summary: bcherny explains Claude Code's deep git worktree support, mentioning he runs dozens of Claudes in parallel using worktrees.
items.reasoning: Key insight into how power users run many parallel Claude sessions. 601 likes. Practical workflow for advanced users.

items.content_html:

Claude Code ships with deep support for git worktrees. Worktrees are essential for doing lots of parallel work in the same repository. I have dozens of Claudes running at all times, and this is how I do it.

Use claude -w to start a new session in a worktree, or hit the "worktree" checkbox in the Claude Desktop app.

For non-git VCS users, use the WorktreeCreate hook to add your own logic for worktree creation.

Learn more: https://t.co/Yv3SlQr1Kr

items.content:
The competition is nuts in AI Agent-running platforms. 

Am talking to a group of Tesla fans and everyone is catching wind that they need to get into this world. 

What is Base44? From the company...

"Base44 is the AI agent platform that actually does things. Build apps, connect tools, automate workflows — just tell your Superagent what to do. It handles database, authentication, hosting, and deployment out of the box. Founded by Maor Shlomo as a solo bootstrapped project, Base44 scaled to 300K+ users and $3.5M ARR in six months before being acquired by Wix for $80M. It is one of the fastest-growing tools in the vibe-coding and no-code space."

items.title: @gabriberton That would only be true if the losses didn’t share any computation. I would think the f...
items.content: @gabriberton That would only be true if the losses didn’t share any computation. I would think the far more common case of multiple losses would be regularizations on a shared set of layers, in which case splitting the loss backwards would still give the same result, but be early twice as slow. PyTorch is actually quite good about avoiding redundant work in backward passes.
items.author: @ID_AA_Carmack
items.summary: John Carmack provides a technical correction about PyTorch backward passes with multiple losses, explaining that splitting losses on shared layers would be redundant and slower, and PyTorch already avoids redundant computation.
items.reasoning: Genuine technical insight from John Carmack (legendary programmer) about PyTorch internals and gradient computation. Low engagement but high technical credibility. Niche but demonstrates deep understanding of ML framework optimization.
items.summary_html: John Carmack provides a technical correction about PyTorch backward passes with multiple losses, explaining that splitting losses on shared layers would be redundant and slower, and PyTorch already avoids redundant computation.
items.content_html: @gabriberton That would only be true if the losses didn’t share any computation. I would think the far more common case of multiple losses would be regularizations on a shared set of layers, in which case splitting the loss backwards would still give the same result, but be early twice as slow. PyTorch is actually quite good about avoiding redundant work in backward passes.

items.title: Thrilled to have Ai2’s VP of Engineering Jeremy Tryba on stage at @GeekWire's Agents of Transformati...
items.content:
Thrilled to have Ai2’s VP of Engineering Jeremy Tryba on stage at @GeekWire's Agents of Transformation event last week. 

He painted a vivid picture of what agentic AI can do for science, and cancer research in particular. 👇 

"When you have an agent building this tree of surprising results, you can have a human oncologist wake up in the morning and say, 'Hey, if that's true, that's actually pretty interesting.' The kinds of things that potentially lead to changes in treatment for different types of cancer."

Asta AutoDiscovery is already impacting how oncologists think about cancer treatment. It works by autonomously generating & testing hypotheses on your data, guided by Bayesian surprise to surface the unexpected, not the obvious. 🔬 

Researchers have already run 35K+ hypotheses across social science, climate science, marine ecology, & more.

🧪 Try it: https://t.co/KeXuo3NIue 
📺 Watch the panel: https://t.co/znmDivx4tm

items.title:
Robotics is about to have its LLM moment.

One of the big issues with industrial robots is having to...
items.content:
Robotics is about to have its LLM moment.

One of the big issues with industrial robots is having to program individual tasks down to the millimeter and hope nothing changes.

Now imagine a foundation model that controls any robot for any task, learning from data rather than being manually programmed.

This could become a flywheel where the more we use the robots, the more data we generate, and the better the robots become.

We have no idea what's coming.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: svpino predicts robotics is about to have its 'LLM moment' with foundation models that can control any robot for any task, creating a data flywheel where more robot usage generates more training data.

items.title:
12/ Use --bare to speed up SDK startup by up to 10x

By default, when you run claude -p (or the Type...
items.content:
12/ Use --bare to speed up SDK startup by up to 10x

By default, when you run claude -p (or the TypeScript or Python SDKs) we search for local CLAUDE.md's, settings, and MCPs.

But for non-interactive usage, most of the time you want to explicitly specify what to load via --system-prompt, --mcp-config, --settings, etc.

This was a design oversight when we first built the SDK, and in a future version, we will flip the default to --bare. For now, opt in with the flag.
items.author: @bcherny
items.summary: bcherny reveals --bare flag speeds up Claude Code SDK startup by up to 10x, noting the current default is a design oversight they plan to fix.
items.reasoning: Notable performance tip with an honest admission of a design oversight. Important for SDK users. 499 likes.
items.summary_html: bcherny reveals --bare flag speeds up Claude Code SDK startup by up to 10x, noting the current default is a design oversight they plan to fix.
items.content_html:
12/ Use --bare to speed up SDK startup by up to 10x

By default, when you run claude -p (or the TypeScript or Python SDKs) we search for local CLAUDE.md's, settings, and MCPs.

But for non-interactive usage, most of the time you want to explicitly specify what to load via --system-prompt, --mcp-config, --settings, etc.

This was a design oversight when we first built the SDK, and in a future version, we will flip the default to --bare. For now, opt in with the flag.

items.title: DeepSeek OCR-2 released in January is much better in quality compared to OCR-1, which made a lot of ...
items.content:
DeepSeek OCR-2 released in January is much better in quality compared to OCR-1, which made a lot of noise when it was released.

OCR-2 not only outputs text but also provides accurate bounding boxes for figures, equations, tables, etc. The quality is impressive for a 3B parameter model.

The architecture is different too and is more similar to how people read, rather than just scanning from left to right and from top to bottom as the previous architecture did.

Read this technical report to see how it was done: https://t.co/HxUn33RiDO
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov reviews DeepSeek OCR-2 (released January), noting it's much better than OCR-1, provides accurate bounding boxes for figures/equations/tables, uses a more human-like reading architecture, and is impressive for a 3B parameter model.
items.reasoning: Useful technical review of DeepSeek OCR-2 with specific architectural insights. Relevant to document understanding and small model capabilities.

items.title:
13/ Use --add-dir to give Claude access to more folders

When working across multiple repositories, ...
items.content:
13/ Use --add-dir to give Claude access to more folders

When working across multiple repositories, I usually start Claude in one repo and use --add-dir (or /add-dir) to let Claude see the other repo. This not only tells Claude about the repo, but also gives it permissions to work in the repo.

Or, add "additionalDirectories" to your team's settings.json to always load in additional folders when starting Claude Code.

https://t.co/HP12BtgFLv

items.title: Previously, you took someone else's open source code that does something very niche, and it only wor...
items.content:
Previously, you took someone else's open source code that does something very niche, and it only works in a specific setup the original coder worked in (like a specific version of Linux running on a specific CPU architecture).

You try to run it on your Mac, it fails, and you are stuck. 

You write to the coder, but they don't respond or respond using terminology you don't understand. You are stuck forever.

Now, you say to Claude Code, "Here's the directory with the code I cannot run. Make it work." And it makes it work. With agentic coders, you are never stuck.

When I hear that vibe coding doesn't increase productivity, I know they're lying. Between being hopelessly stuck forever and never being stuck, there's infinite growth in productivity.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov describes a common open-source code portability problem and how Claude Code solves it by making previously-stuck situations solvable, arguing this represents infinite productivity growth.
items.reasoning: Compelling real-world example of AI coding agent utility. Good engagement (118 likes). The specific use case of making incompatible open-source code work on different platforms resonates with many developers.
items.summary_html: Burkov describes a common open-source code portability problem and how Claude Code solves it by making previously-stuck situations solvable, arguing this represents infinite productivity growth.
items.content_html:
Previously, you took someone else's open source code that does something very niche, and it only works in a specific setup the original coder worked in (like a specific version of Linux running on a specific CPU architecture).

You try to run it on your Mac, it fails, and you are stuck.

You write to the coder, but they don't respond or respond using terminology you don't understand. You are stuck forever.

Now, you say to Claude Code, "Here's the directory with the code I cannot run. Make it work." And it makes it work. With agentic coders, you are never stuck.

When I hear that vibe coding doesn't increase productivity, I know they're lying. Between being hopelessly stuck forever and never being stuck, there's infinite growth in productivity.

items.title:
This could be powerful. 

An agent trace captures the full execution of an AI agent — the tool calls...
items.content:
This could be powerful. 

An agent trace captures the full execution of an AI agent — the tool calls it made, the reasoning steps, inputs/outputs at each step, errors, retries, etc.

The datasets matter because
1. you can use them to benchmark agent behavior, study failure modes, and researchers better models
2. Right now most agent traces are proprietary (inside companies building agents or model providers like Anthropic, OpenAI, Google)

items.summary_html: Aparna Dhinakaran discusses the importance of agent trace datasets - capturing full execution of AI agents including tool calls, reasoning, errors. Notes most traces are proprietary and open datasets would enable benchmarking and failure mode analysis.
items.content_html:
This could be powerful.

An agent trace captures the full execution of an AI agent — the tool calls it made, the reasoning steps, inputs/outputs at each step, errors, retries, etc.

The datasets matter because

1. you can use them to benchmark agent behavior, study failure modes, and researchers better models

2. Right now most agent traces are proprietary (inside companies building agents or model providers like Anthropic, OpenAI, Google)

items.title: @emollick it’s certainly not “HUGE” by conventional interpretations; in traditional psychology (Jaco...
items.content:
@emollick it’s certainly not “HUGE” by conventional interpretations; in traditional psychology (Jacob Cohen’s d) .15 is small; even in the context of education I am skeptical, and see quote below from Interpreting Effect Sizes of Education Interventions
Matthew A. Kraft), which would still count it as medium, not large and certainly not all caps HUGE.

But th thing about effect sizes of 0.15 in my experience is that there is usually enough of a confidence interval around it that you can’t be sure how real it is. 

That was Cohen’s point. 

(source for the 6-9 months?)
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Marcus disputes Emollick's characterization of 0.15 SD effect size as 'HUGE' in education, citing Cohen's conventions and arguing it's small with unclear significance.
items.reasoning: Substantive methodological debate between two prominent voices about AI's educational impact. Important for understanding evidence quality around AI in education.

items.title: A lot of people mistake inadequate scaffolding for a lack of capability in smaller local models. You...
items.content:
A lot of people mistake inadequate scaffolding for a lack of capability in smaller local models. You can't expect a system that was built for large model APIs to work out of the box magically with smaller local models.

Similarly, many people fine-tune poorly and then conclude that fine-tuning does not work.

There are clearly still major skills gaps and time investment constraints with local model currently but I'm quite hopeful that a mix of open-source explosion, community work and agent support will make it drastically easier. 

And then we'll witness the open-source and local explosion that is so necessary!

There are clearly still major skills gaps and time investment constraints with local model currently but I'm quite hopeful that a mix of open-source explosion, community work and agent support will make it drastically easier.

And then we'll witness the open-source and local explosion that is so necessary!

items.title:
VR is dead.

Except it has 1,000 lives.

You may not call what happens next year VR.

But to me VR=i...
items.content:
VR is dead.

Except it has 1,000 lives.

You may not call what happens next year VR.

But to me VR=infinite realities. Includes reality itself.

A metaverse?

Small thinking.

The glasses will be able to change reality itself.

Or your perception there in.

Remember I wore my Apple Vision Pro on my first @Waymo ride.

Real reality wasn’t available.

But the reality I saw inside sure looked convincing to me.

It is just too heavy.

When it gets light I will tell everyone to buy one.

Why do I still believe?

Because VR is magic. Just not magic enough to get over a heavy headset.

But lighter ones are coming.

And world models will cut the cost for creating for it greatly.

Content is the magic.

Soon you will be inside movies.

Wanna be an actor?

You will be anyway whether or not you want it.

Which is a little dystopian.

Neal: we have to talk.

What will we call it if we don’t call it VR anymore?

The Holodeck.

Soon not just the reader interface but the entire UI will have three color modes.

There's arguably no better UI for reading scientific or technical content than ChapterPal.

items.title:
This new paper fixes one of the biggest limitation in self-improving AI.

Self-improving AI is not n...
items.content:
This new paper fixes one of the biggest limitation in self-improving AI.

Self-improving AI is not new, but the upgrade process stayed fixed and it only worked in coding. 

A new paper introduces Hyperagents. 

The system splits into two editable parts:
1. A task solver for the problem
2. A meta solver that rewrites both

The second part evolves how new agents are created. 

Then the improved process builds stronger versions. 

It was tested across four domains:

1. Coding
2. Academic paper review
3. Robotics reward design
4. Math solution grading 

It beat every baseline, including the previous best self-improving system. 

Meta-level gains transfer across domains and accumulate across runs. 

A trick learned in one domain made the others improve faster.

The system doesn't just find better solutions. 

It keeps improving its ability to search for them.

items.content_html:
This new paper fixes one of the biggest limitation in self-improving AI.

Self-improving AI is not new, but the upgrade process stayed fixed and it only worked in coding.

A new paper introduces Hyperagents.

The system splits into two editable parts:

1. A task solver for the problem

2. A meta solver that rewrites both

The second part evolves how new agents are created.

Then the improved process builds stronger versions.

It was tested across four domains:

1. Coding

2. Academic paper review

3. Robotics reward design

4. Math solution grading

It beat every baseline, including the previous best self-improving system.

Meta-level gains transfer across domains and accumulate across runs.

A trick learned in one domain made the others improve faster.

The system doesn't just find better solutions.

It keeps improving its ability to search for them.

items.title: 🇵🇹 If you have ever dealt with any Portuguese business (and I have with many in my 5 years here) you...
items.content:
🇵🇹 If you have ever dealt with any Portuguese business (and I have with many in my 5 years here) you know why

Portuguese in Portugal simply do not care about work, some foreigners find that refreshing, but it results in a country where you can't find anyone to do work because nobody wants to work

And when they do work, the work is generally bad, like imagine stepping into a time machine back to 1970s bad, they also can't follow the spec you agreed upon beforehand, and then when it's 60% done they disappear and ghost you

When we did home renovatiom, you hire ppl, they arrive late at 9am (you agreed 8am), they then drive away to get materials, come back 11am, do actual work for an hour, then at 12noon announce they go for lunch, come back around 2.30pm, walk around a bit, do some work, and then leave early at 4pm

It's not just construction workers, it's almost every interaction you have here with a Portuguese business, the quality is just low, the service non-existent or customer-hostile, and it's not the language, I speak fluent Portuguese, I'm respectful and friendly which is why I never ever had this amount of issues elsewhere in the world

And I know it's not just my experience, it's everyone I know here, even Portuguese complain about Portuguese!

It's not completely their fault though, the hostile tax system for both people and businesses literally gives you an incentive to never ever scale your business beyond I believe around €150,000/year, because you end up in a completely different category and tax tier that decimates you with more taxes and more bookkeeping

So every business tries to stay small, and doesn't want more customers (how many times have I walked into a Portuguese cafe or shop and the staff or owner *sighed* "not another customer")

Add decades of socialist governments that hand out free money to 50% of the country (and even the right wing parties here are socialist btw, they have to be or they don't get votes) and you don't have any incentive to work left

Why work when you don't need to work?

The majority of the young smart Portuguese people with actual ambitions understandably move elsewhere, because if there's no incentive to grow a business, there's also no jobs for them, and the whole thing becomes a vicious cycle of increasing poverty

Which is why I said Portuguese in Portugal at the beginning of this tweet, because Portuguese outside of Portugal are ambitious, want to work, want to improve their lives!

Bringing in lots of foreigners like me by the government as a way to pump money into the system, we spend a lot and it pays for the welfare, healthcare and retirements of Portuguese now, but it's not a structural solution (and we also instantly became the scapegoat for the government's decades of mismanagement)

It'd be relatively easy to fix this though if people in Portugal would put on their thinking hat:

💡 Make Portugal a great place to start and run a business, model yourself after Singapore but with extremely low taxes (maybe 5-10%) for companies/startups and make it easy for them to hire (and fire) people, the money you lose in taxes you will get back in increased economic activity over time (but it will hurt for a little bit)

Because even the foreigners who move here and start businesses get caught up in the reverse incentive spiral of Portugal:

Why even work?

items.content:
things that seem possible but are hard to one-shot* with the latest agents

- come up a unique tee concept, design it, and sell at least 100 tee shirts for a profit
- sell $1000 worth of drop shipped goods for a profit including customer acquisition cost
- create three social accounts with at least 10k followers each
- create an account on a freelance marketplace and complete three jobs end to end til payment
- create and manage a daily podcast with at least one regular paying sponsor

*by one-shot, I mean the agent figures out which tools/sites it requires, and creates accounts on their own, handles all communication with ppl

items.content:
@SkarredGhost VR is dead.  

Except it has 1,000 lives.  

You may not call what happens next year VR.  

But to me VR=infinite realities. Includes reality itself.  

A metaverse?  Small thinking.  

The glasses will be able to change reality itself.  

Or your perception there in.  

Remember I wore my Apple Vision Pro on my first @Waymo ride.  

Real reality wasn’t available.  

But the reality I saw inside sure looked convincing to me. 

 It is just too heavy.  

When it gets light I will tell everyone to buy one.  

Why do I still believe?  

Because VR is magic. Just not magic enough to get over a heavy headset.  But lighter ones are coming.  

And world models will cut the cost for creating for it greatly.  

Content is the magic.  

Soon you will be inside movies.  

Wanna be an actor?  

You will be anyway whether or not you want it.  

Which is a little dystopian.  N

eal: we have to talk.  

What will we call it if we don’t call it VR anymore?  

The Holodeck.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer writes an extended reflection on the future of VR, arguing it's not dead but needs lighter headsets, world models to cut content costs, and will evolve into 'The Holodeck.' References Apple Vision Pro, Waymo rides, and AI-generated content for VR.
items.reasoning: Thoughtful long-form speculation connecting AI world models to VR content generation. The thesis that world models will dramatically reduce VR content creation costs is interesting and connects AI progress to spatial computing's future.

• You sign up

You can start asking your agent to d...
items.content:
Here's a simple summary:

• You sign up
• You are ready to go!

You can start asking your agent to do work for you right away.

The highlights:

1. Obviously, the zero-setup model is amazing. This is the biggest difference compared to OpenClaw.

2. You get a sandboxed AI environment. Fully isolated execution with role-based access control.

3. Your credentials go to an encrypted vault. Your API keys and passwords aren't floating around in plaintext.

4. Long context memory. @Pokee_AI mentions they have a very long memory window.

5. 70% lower token consumption than Openclaw. This adds up fast when you're running agents continuously.

Check it out here: https://t.co/2rhlGyWCah

Thanks to the @Pokee_AI team for partnering with me on this post.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Santiago (svpino) details PokeeClaw features: zero-setup agent platform with sandboxed execution, encrypted vault, long context memory, and 70% lower token consumption than OpenClaw

items.content_html:
Here's a simple summary:

• You sign up

• You are ready to go!

You can start asking your agent to do work for you right away.

The highlights:

1. Obviously, the zero-setup model is amazing. This is the biggest difference compared to OpenClaw.

2. You get a sandboxed AI environment. Fully isolated execution with role-based access control.

3. Your credentials go to an encrypted vault. Your API keys and passwords aren't floating around in plaintext.

4. Long context memory. @Pokee_AI mentions they have a very long memory window.

5. 70% lower token consumption than Openclaw. This adds up fast when you're running agents continuously.

Check it out here: https://t.co/2rhlGyWCah

Thanks to the @Pokee_AI team for partnering with me on this post.

items.content:
@r0ck3t23 Yes, we need a sea change in America. 

But generally people only change a few ways:

1. Pain.
2. Death.
3. Spiritual awakening (IE education and curiosity). 

America is gonna have a tough time keeping up and that's not a spot it likes to be in. But structurally our country needs a massive overhaul, particularly in education, and we just don't have the right leadership to get us there.

Elon being one of the bright spots.

items.title: 🏆The Kaggle Book — Master Data Analysis and Data Science Competitions with Machine Learning, GenAI, ...
items.content:
🏆The Kaggle Book — Master Data Analysis and Data Science Competitions with Machine Learning, GenAI, & LLMs [2nd Edition]: https://t.co/ZjNKsvFl06

𝗧𝗮𝗯𝗹𝗲 𝗼𝗳 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗻𝘁𝘀:
🔶Introducing Data Science Competition
🔷Organizing Data with Datasets
🔶Work & Learn with Kaggle Notebooks
🔷Kaggle Models
🔶Leveraging Discussion Forums
🔷Detailing Competition Tasks & Metrics
🔶Designing Good Validation Schemes
🔷Modeling for Tabular Competitions
🔶Hyperparameter Optimization
🔷Ensembling & Stacking Solutions
🔶Modeling Image Classification & Segmentation

𝗠𝘆 𝗥𝗲𝘃𝗶𝗲𝘄 (𝗼𝗻 𝗔𝗺𝗮𝘇𝗼𝗻):
This 700-page masterpiece of writing covers everything you need—start to finish—to be a competitive coder, specifically for Kaggle data science competitions. The book covers the mechanics of the competitions (platform, resources, rankings, leaderboards), then the infrastructure (notebooks, GitHub, data sets, frameworks, discussion forums), and then nearly 500 pages devoted to "Elevating Your Game" (in-depth coverage of modeling techniques, evaluation metrics, validation strategies, hyperparameter optimization, ensembles, stacking, and various categories of competitions: tabular data, computer vision, NLP, Gen AI, simulations). The book concludes with a valuable section on building your Kaggle portfolio for career advancement and new opportunities. This is an outstanding data science / AI / Machine Learning training resource for anyone, even if you are not into the competitions, though especially if you are a dedicated Kaggler.

The 2026 RSAC conference last week was full of great announcements, demos, presentations, and the ResOps Rumble presented by @Commvault. ResOps is #Commvault's term for Unified Resilience Operations, where traditionally siloed security, infrastructure, and IT operations teams work together to tackle threat hunting, resilience, investigation, and clean recovery operations across the enterprise — with an emphasis on 𝗰𝗹𝗲𝗮𝗻 𝗿𝗲𝗰𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘆. This unified operating model enables and secures a continuous, automated system to maintain business continuity during attacks, outages, and AI-driven disruptions.

@Commvault had several impactful announcements at RSAC and in the weeks leading up to the event. Below is a list of some of these announcements. Find these and more in the Commvault Newsroom here: https://t.co/ytmVOpT4uJ

⚛️"NetApp and Commvault Advance Cyber Resilience with Strategic Alliance"

⚛️"Commvault Connects AI Threat Detection, Investigation, and Trusted Recovery with Microsoft Security"

⚛️"Commvault and TIME Launch Inaugural CISO of the Year Award to Recognize Cyber Resilience Leaders"

⚛️"Commvault Extends Enterprise Resilience to Structured and AI Data with Real-Time Governance Controls"

⚛️"Commvault Expands Threat Scan with Layered Threat Detection to Advance Verified Clean Recoveries"

⚛️"Commvault and CloudSEK Join Forces to Address a Growing and Rapidly Emerging Identity Threat: Exposed Credentials on the Dark Web"

⚛️"Commvault Extends Identity Resilience to Okta Amid Exploding Identity-Driven Cyber Risk"

⚛️"Commvault Expands Unified Resilience with Bi-Directional Integration with CrowdStrike Falcon Next-Gen SIEM"

⚛️"Commvault Unveils a New Era in Enterprise Resilience with the Commvault Cloud Unity Platform Release"

⚛️"Commvault Ranked Highest in Five of Six Use Cases in the 2025 Gartner® Critical Capabilities for Backup and Data Protection Platforms"

@Commvault had several impactful announcements at RSAC and in the weeks leading up to the event. Below is a list of some of these announcements. Find these and more in the Commvault Newsroom here: https://t.co/ytmVOpT4uJ

⚛️"NetApp and Commvault Advance Cyber Resilience with Strategic Alliance"

⚛️"Commvault Connects AI Threat Detection, Investigation, and Trusted Recovery with Microsoft Security"

⚛️"Commvault and TIME Launch Inaugural CISO of the Year Award to Recognize Cyber Resilience Leaders"

⚛️"Commvault Extends Enterprise Resilience to Structured and AI Data with Real-Time Governance Controls"

⚛️"Commvault Expands Threat Scan with Layered Threat Detection to Advance Verified Clean Recoveries"

⚛️"Commvault and CloudSEK Join Forces to Address a Growing and Rapidly Emerging Identity Threat: Exposed Credentials on the Dark Web"

⚛️"Commvault Extends Identity Resilience to Okta Amid Exploding Identity-Driven Cyber Risk"

⚛️"Commvault Expands Unified Resilience with Bi-Directional Integration with CrowdStrike Falcon Next-Gen SIEM"

⚛️"Commvault Unveils a New Era in Enterprise Resilience with the Commvault Cloud Unity Platform Release"

⚛️"Commvault Ranked Highest in Five of Six Use Cases in the 2025 Gartner® Critical Capabilities for Backup and Data Protection Platforms"

items.title:
The bigger opportunity is APIs.

We saw 4G enable Uber, Grubhub, the app economy.

Now 5G + network ...
items.content:
The bigger opportunity is APIs.

We saw 4G enable Uber, Grubhub, the app economy.

Now 5G + network APIs enable developers to build applications that assume guaranteed performance.

That is a fundamental shift.

When performance becomes programmable, telcos move up the value chain.

From connectivity providers → to experience platforms.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon

items.summary_html: Ronald van Loon argues APIs are the bigger 5G opportunity, comparing how 4G enabled Uber/app economy and 5G network APIs will enable programmable performance.
items.content_html:
The bigger opportunity is APIs.

We saw 4G enable Uber, Grubhub, the app economy.

Now 5G + network APIs enable developers to build applications that assume guaranteed performance.

That is a fundamental shift.

When performance becomes programmable, telcos move up the value chain.

From connectivity providers → to experience platforms.

items.title: Great Quantum Time Arrow : Time could flow both backward, forward in great quantum realm time arrow,...
items.content:
Great Quantum Time Arrow : Time could flow both backward, forward in great quantum realm time arrow, suggests study.

Scientists utilized sophisticated mathematical models to illustrate the evolution of a quantum system’s state over time. Surprisingly, they discovered that the fundamental equations governing this evolution remained invariant, whether time progressed forward or reversed. This remarkable symmetry challenges our understanding of time itself and underscores the profound complexities inherent in quantum mechanics.

In our lived reality, we typically perceive time as a linear progression moving in single direction, where today seamlessly transitions into tomorrow and the days that follow. However, exciting new research in the quantum realm suggests that time may flow in opposite directions as well. This enlightening discovery offers us a deeper understanding of the nature of time and encourages us to reevaluate our conventional perspectives, highlighting the complexities and wonders of the quantum world.

A groundbreaking study conducted by scientists from the School of Mathematics and Physics at the University of Surrey in the UK delved into the intricacies of time within quantum systems through sophisticated mathematical equations. To their astonishment, they uncovered the existence of opposing arrows of time, which serve as compelling theoretical evidence of time's bizarre and multifaceted flow at the quantum level. This significant discovery challenges our conventional understanding of time, revealing that it may not adhere to the linear progression we so often take for granted. Instead, it invites us to confront the possibility of a far more complex and enigmatic reality, fundamentally transforming our perspective on the nature of time itself.

“Our findings suggest that, although our everyday experience leads us to believe that time progresses in a single direction, the opposite flow has equally viable potential,” stated Andrea Rocco, A study's authors and an associate professor at the University of Surrey. This insight underscores the profound complexities of time, urging us to reconsider not only how we perceive its passage but also the underlying principles that govern it in the quantum realm.

References

Brahambhatt, R. (2025, February 15). Great realm: Time could flow both backward, forward in quantum realm, suggests study. Interesting Engineering. https://t.co/WZUknK2kfU

Here is the APA 7 citation for the article, following your preferences:

Strömberg, T., Schiansky, P., Quintino, M. T., Antesberger, M., Rozema, L., Agresti, I., Brukner, C., & Walther, P. (2024, May 14). Superposition of a quantum evolution with its time reverse. arXiv. https://t.co/cEREYkSuDS

items.title: Playing DOOM in 2026 is honestly boring but at least it lets you instantly play without an hour intr...
items.content:
Playing DOOM in 2026 is honestly boring but at least it lets you instantly play without an hour intro of cut scenes and tutorials and then when you load it a second time downloading a 50GB update for half an hour 

Modern games really just suck and it's no wonder the industry is suffering:
- Revenue is down after inflation
- Player counts are down
- Studios are doing massive layoffs

They've ignored what people want for the last decade and now they're seeing it in their numbers

items.title:
I'm not sending anyone my passport anymore

My Portuguese lawyer wanted me to email her a copy of my...
items.content:
I'm not sending anyone my passport anymore

My Portuguese lawyer wanted me to email her a copy of my passport for KYC

I rejected and she was confused

"I've never been hacked"

99% of people are not aware any account probably can and will be hacked on a long enough timespan

The best security is NOT storing sensitive data ever

items.title:
Like the logical step is for @Stripe to be not just a payment service but an actual fintech

I have ...
items.content:
Like the logical step is for @Stripe to be not just a payment service but an actual fintech

I have no idea why they're not doing that

It makes logical sense my business bank account (Wise) is ALSO the place I accept payments from my startup's customers with a checkout page integrated into my site (Stripe)

Stripe isn't offering cards or bank accounts (yes they keep announcing it but where is my Stripe card or bank account then)

Wise isn't offering proper checkout pages for my customers

Others like Airwallex are starting to offer both

items.title:
5G network slicing is the unlock.

It allows operators to create purpose-built slices of the network...
items.content:
5G network slicing is the unlock.

It allows operators to create purpose-built slices of the network for specific use cases:

→ Ultra-low latency for gaming
→ High uplink quality for live streaming
→ Secure, fraud-protected transactions for retail
→ Immersive AR and VR experiences

This is not theoretical.

Operators in Asia are already monetizing event-based passes, four-day upgrades, even influencer packages.

items.title:
Energy is back at the center of global attention.

LNG markets, supply risks, and rising demand are ...
items.content:
Energy is back at the center of global attention.

LNG markets, supply risks, and rising demand are testing power systems, making reliability, affordability, and resilience the real priority ahead of events like Gastech 2026.

More> https://t.co/xCkI03MinV

@GastechEvent Partner.

The real question today is no longer just how much energy we can produce. It is how to build systems that remain reliable, affordable, and resilient under increasing pressure.

This is where Gastech 2026 becomes relevant.

From 14–17 September in Bangkok, governments, energy companies, investors, and technology leaders will come together to work on practical solutions, from LNG and supply security to hydrogen, climate tech, more flexible grids, and digital optimization of energy systems.

A step forward in building more secure, affordable, and innovative energy systems for the years ahead.

items.title:
All I hear from Stripe is stablecoin this that

Very interesting but as a business I don't care

I j...
items.content:
All I hear from Stripe is stablecoin this that

Very interesting but as a business I don't care

I just want a payment card I can use to pay my server bills WITH the money from my customers that's inside Stripe

Why do I have to take it out of Stripe as a payout at all? Why does it need to go to Wise who takes a cut and then I pay Hetzner for my server bill?

Why not Stripe balance to Hetzner bill? And if you build that out long enough you can remove the expensive intermediary of payment cards (Visa/MC) too
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio proposes Stripe should enable direct balance-to-vendor payments, eliminating intermediaries like Wise and Visa/MC. 164K views, 925 likes.
items.reasoning: Very high engagement fintech discussion but not AI-related
items.summary_html: Levelsio proposes Stripe should enable direct balance-to-vendor payments, eliminating intermediaries like Wise and Visa/MC. 164K views, 925 likes.
items.content_html:
All I hear from Stripe is stablecoin this that

Very interesting but as a business I don't care

I just want a payment card I can use to pay my server bills WITH the money from my customers that's inside Stripe

Why do I have to take it out of Stripe as a payout at all? Why does it need to go to Wise who takes a cut and then I pay Hetzner for my server bill?

Why not Stripe balance to Hetzner bill? And if you build that out long enough you can remove the expensive intermediary of payment cards (Visa/MC) too

items.title:
I think @Stripe needs to add payment cards ASAP to spend your balance

Because I still don't have on...
items.content:
I think @Stripe needs to add payment cards ASAP to spend your balance

Because I still don't have one and others are adding them fast

Once the other fintechs also add checkout options (like @Airwallex has) there's a real switching opportunity for a lot of people on Stripe

Stripe is the incumbent now and every fintech is sneaking up on them

But they're moving too slow

I don't have a dog in this fight, I prefer stay with Stripe as always but competitors are starting to offer more things now

items.title:
We are watching a shift from selling bandwidth → to selling outcomes.

Speed is table stakes.

Perfo...
items.content:
We are watching a shift from selling bandwidth → to selling outcomes.

Speed is table stakes.

Performance is premium.

Think about a World Cup stadium:

→ Ordering food from your seat
→ Streaming live as an influencer
→ Placing secure mobile payments
→ Playing real-time AR experiences

In those moments, latency and reliability matter more than raw Mbps.

That is where differentiated connectivity comes in.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon

Related I love Brazil but it does an absolutely terrible job marketing itself to the world

All foreigners know about Brazil is favelas, carnaval and football, that's what I knew before too as a European coming here in the last 3 years

The reality is completely different

My friend visiting now "my dream is to visit the favela in Rio"

I have to explain that no the wish of Brazilians is not to visit a favela

There's much more beautiful things in Brazil than people robbing and murdering innocent people on the street every day

(But I do like baile funk)

items.title:
It's always a great time to tell everyone modern Airbus planes are now the safest in the world

None...
items.content:
It's always a great time to tell everyone modern Airbus planes are now the safest in the world

None of the modern models have had any fatal accidents including:
- Airbus A319 Neo
- Airbus A320 Neo
- Airbus A321 Neo
- Airbus A330 Neo
- Airbus A340
- Airbus A350
- Airbus A380

But the model in this video is actually an older Airbus A330 CEO, the previous series from 1994, it oddly has a pretty bad safety record in my opinion: 14 hull losses

Before booking flights you can usually see the plane model, I try choose modern Airbus models when possible
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio detailed post about modern Airbus planes' safety records, listing models with zero fatalities and warning about older A330 CEO
items.reasoning: High engagement (90K views) but entirely about aviation safety, no AI content
items.summary_html: Levelsio detailed post about modern Airbus planes' safety records, listing models with zero fatalities and warning about older A330 CEO
items.content_html:
It's always a great time to tell everyone modern Airbus planes are now the safest in the world

None of the modern models have had any fatal accidents including:

  • Airbus A319 Neo
  • Airbus A320 Neo
  • Airbus A321 Neo
  • Airbus A330 Neo
  • Airbus A340
  • Airbus A350
  • Airbus A380

But the model in this video is actually an older Airbus A330 CEO, the previous series from 1994, it oddly has a pretty bad safety record in my opinion: 14 hull losses

Before booking flights you can usually see the plane model, I try choose modern Airbus models when possible

items.title:
Math for Web Design — Precision Layout and Animation: https://t.co/TVdqIKRmka

Amazon summary:

Beau...
items.content:
Math for Web Design — Precision Layout and Animation: https://t.co/TVdqIKRmka

Amazon summary:

Beautiful websites and apps are rooted in established mathematical principles. Know the math, and you'll gain a superpower when it comes to layout calculations, animating page elements, accurate color rendering, and responsive design. “Math for Web Design” teaches you the math you need to create awesome, precise, and highly functional front-end designs.

You'll learn how to: 

- Improve layouts and animations with the Golden Ratio and proportional scaling.
- Optimize responsiveness and accessibility with percentage-based sizing.
- Create UI effects with physics, vectors, and geometric transformations.
- Master algebra and grid calculations for CSS Grid and Flexbox.
- Grok color theory and blending math.

items.title: For the first time in history, the American president honestly says what the true reasons for a war ...
items.content:
For the first time in history, the American president honestly says what the true reasons for a war against a sovereign nation are, but when the next US president comes to power, they will go back to telling people lies about bringing democracy to Neanderthals, and people will be happy to believe that Trump was just weird and that America isn't just invading countries for oil and natural resources like a gangster.

All evil regimes pretend they are the good guys and claim they are fighting evil regimes. Saying that some of them are better than others is like distinguishing between sorts of shit.

European bureaucrats are especially connoisseurs of the sorts of shit.

items.summary: Burkov makes political commentary about American foreign policy and European bureaucrats.

items.summary_html: Burkov makes political commentary about American foreign policy and European bureaucrats.
items.content_html:
For the first time in history, the American president honestly says what the true reasons for a war against a sovereign nation are, but when the next US president comes to power, they will go back to telling people lies about bringing democracy to Neanderthals, and people will be happy to believe that Trump was just weird and that America isn't just invading countries for oil and natural resources like a gangster.

All evil regimes pretend they are the good guys and claim they are fighting evil regimes. Saying that some of them are better than others is like distinguishing between sorts of shit.

European bureaucrats are especially connoisseurs of the sorts of shit.

items.title: 🇧🇷 Another Brazilian buffet, I didn't want to be annoying making a video because kinda busy but this...
items.content:
🇧🇷 Another Brazilian buffet, I didn't want to be annoying making a video because kinda busy but this one extra interesting because it was at the airport in Viracopos near Sao Paulo

It's Azul Airlines' main airport, Azul means blue and is the same founder and CEO as JetBlue in America, his name @davidneeleman

He's also interesting because his grandfather was born to Dutch immigrants in Utah!

But then he himself was born in Sao Paulo, and lived there until he was 5 years old, then he moved to the US where he founded JetBlue, WestJet and a few more airlines

Later he came back to Brazil to start Azul which quickly became the biggest airline here

It's similar to JetBlue, it's not luxury but also not cheap, it's comfortable but basic, but with great service etc.

Anyway back to the buffet, it's great the airport has a buffet cause it means you can eat healthy which is always a big problem when you're traveling

Today I had steak, chicken, eggs, potatoes, some rice and beans and tomatoand cucumber with sparkling water

BOM APETITE 🇧🇷

items.content_html:
🇧🇷 Another Brazilian buffet, I didn't want to be annoying making a video because kinda busy but this one extra interesting because it was at the airport in Viracopos near Sao Paulo

It's Azul Airlines' main airport, Azul means blue and is the same founder and CEO as JetBlue in America, his name @davidneeleman

He's also interesting because his grandfather was born to Dutch immigrants in Utah!

But then he himself was born in Sao Paulo, and lived there until he was 5 years old, then he moved to the US where he founded JetBlue, WestJet and a few more airlines

Later he came back to Brazil to start Azul which quickly became the biggest airline here

It's similar to JetBlue, it's not luxury but also not cheap, it's comfortable but basic, but with great service etc.

Anyway back to the buffet, it's great the airport has a buffet cause it means you can eat healthy which is always a big problem when you're traveling

Today I had steak, chicken, eggs, potatoes, some rice and beans and tomatoand cucumber with sparkling water

BOM APETITE 🇧🇷

items.title:
Commercial planes are designed for engines to be able to go on fire and keep flying

They cut off th...
items.content:
Commercial planes are designed for engines to be able to go on fire and keep flying

They cut off the fuel, hydraulics and electrics immediately, pump it with fire retardant and that existinguishes the fire in most cases

And then you declare an emergency and return to land
